coredata.dc:title: Biological synthesis of novel carbon quantum dots using Halimeda opuntia green algae with improved optical properties and electrochemical performance for possible energy storage applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Ghamdi, S.A.
Darwish, A.A.A.
Hamdalla, Taymour A.
Pasha, Apsar
Elnair, Mohamed E.
Al-Atawi, Ahmed
Khasim, Syed
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots, known to be nanomaterials with zero dimension have recently emerged as excellent materials for versatile technological applications in optoelectronics, energy storage and conversions. They possess simple and low-cost synthesis methods with outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, high biocompatibility with favourable charge transfer and electronic conductivity. Recently, much attention has been paid towards the eco-friendly synthesis of carbon dots from sources such as biomass as the precursors which provides solution to global challenges of waste management. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted greater attention as energy storage materials because of their unique electrical properties and ability to host various functional groups on the electrode surface. Recently CQDs have been utilized as electrode materials towards enhancing the energy density of supercapacitors. Herein we report the biological synthesis of CQDs using biomass such as green algae of Halimeda opuntia. The obtained CQDs were characterized by High Resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform-Infra Red (FT-IR) spectroscopy, UV-Vis Spectroscopy. The fluorescence properties were analysed through emission spectra. The electrochemical performance of CQDs as a supercapacitor were studied using modified Glassy Carbon Electrode (GCE). The CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia green algae exhibits a specific capacitance of 311 F g−1. Due to excellent optical properties and electrochemical performances, these CQDs synthesized using Halimeda opuntia could potentially be used in energy storage and conversion applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Halimeda opuntia
Green algae
Biosynthesis of CQDs
Energy storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-QD from Halimeda opuntia
Preparation of carbon quantum dots Modified Glassy Carbon Electrode
Characterization of CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of Halimeda opuntia green algae and CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia. (b) XRD pattern of CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia green algae.
(a) and (b) HR-TEM images of CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia green algae and their corresponding (c) Zeta potential.

(a) UV-Vis spectra of CQDs suspension (b) Excitation and emission spectra of CQDs (λex = 285 nm, λem = 422 nm) (c) Emission spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 360 nm.

(a) CV curves for CQDs modified GCE electrode at different scanning rates (10–50 mV/s) in 6 M KOH solution for potential window − 0.4 to 0.4 V (b) Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) for CQDs modified GCE electrode.
Charging-Discharging cycles for CQDs modified GCE electrode for 200 cycles and 10 cycles of operation at 1 A g−1.

(a) Specific capacitance of CQDs/GCE electrodes Vs Current density (b) Specific capacitance of CQDs/GCE electrodes Vs Scan rate (c) Capacitance retention and coulombic efficiency for 5000 cycles of operation.
Performance of biosynthesized carbon-based materials for supercapacitor applications in recent literature.

row.entry: Hydrothermal treatment (180 °C, 48 h), KOH activation (700 °C, 1 h)
Hydrothermal treatment (120 °C, 5 h) Carbonization with (NH4)2HPO4 (800 °C, 3 h)
One pot pyrolysis in NH3 atmosphere and activation with K2CO3 and K2C2O4
Biomass impregnation with NH4H2PO4 and pyrolysis (500 °C, 1.5 h
Carbonization and activation with NaOH (900 °C, 3 h)
Hydrothermal treatment (160 °C, 5 h) and activation with H3PO4 (700 °C, 1 h)
Impregnation with seawater and drying, carbonization (900 °C, 1 h)
Carbonization and biochar activation with KOH (700 °C, 1 h)

head.ce:title: Biological synthesis of novel carbon quantum dots using Halimeda opuntia green algae with improved optical properties and electrochemical performance for possible energy storage applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mathematics, Faculty of Science, University of Tabuk, Tabuk 71491, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots, known to be nanomaterials with zero dimension have recently emerged as excellent materials for versatile technological applications in optoelectronics, energy storage and conversions. They possess simple and low-cost synthesis methods with outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, high biocompatibility with favourable charge transfer and electronic conductivity. Recently, much attention has been paid towards the eco-friendly synthesis of carbon dots from sources such as biomass as the precursors which provides solution to global challenges of waste management. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted greater attention as energy storage materials because of their unique electrical properties and ability to host various functional groups on the electrode surface. Recently CQDs have been utilized as electrode materials towards enhancing the energy density of supercapacitors. Herein we report the biological synthesis of CQDs using biomass such as green algae of Halimeda opuntia. The obtained CQDs were characterized by High Resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform-Infra Red (FT-IR) spectroscopy, UV-Vis Spectroscopy. The fluorescence properties were analysed through emission spectra. The electrochemical performance of CQDs as a supercapacitor were studied using modified Glassy Carbon Electrode (GCE). The CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia green algae exhibits a specific capacitance of 311 F g−1. Due to excellent optical properties and electrochemical performances, these CQDs synthesized using Halimeda opuntia could potentially be used in energy storage and conversion applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Ever growing demands for energy conversion and storage, and fossil fuel depletion, have led to exploration of alternative renewables energy storage and conversion technologies such as Li-ion/Sodium-ion/metal-air batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells [1–3]. Although supercapacitors have demonstrated several advantages as energy storage devices with superior high-power density, faster charging/discharging rate, and long-term cyclic stability [4,5], nevertheless these supercapacitors-based energy storage devices have drawback due to low energy density compared to the conventional Li-ion batteries [6]. Thus, designing novel materials for fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors and batteries through low cost is using biological synthesis techniques is important for overcoming these existing challenges. Carbon nanomaterials, due to their excellent physical and chemical properties along with high electrical conductivity, high aspect ratio, and superior chemical inertness have made them suitable materials for electrode fabrication [7]. Among most widely investigated carbon materials, graphene, and carbon nanotubes (CNT) have displayed improved electrical conductivity, low electron transfer resistance and increased dispersion of active materials on electrode surface [8]. However, the large-scale synthesis of these carbon-based nanomaterials are still relatively expensive and there is a need for the development of novel synthesis methods of these carbon materials at low cost using the biomass available in the environment. In comparison to various other carbon-based materials, carbon quantum dots have relatively ultrasmall sizes, can form uniform dispersions in general solvents, superior electron transfer characteristics, structural features, enhanced quantum effects and photoluminescent properties [9,10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), one of the most emerging members from the family of carbon materials was first reported in 2004 [11]. These CQDs, with dimensions smaller than 10 nm, are mainly composed of amorphous carbon structures along with sp2 hybridized nanocrystalline regions of graphitic carbon. The structure of CQDs possess unique size and tuneable physical, chemical, electronic and optical properties. They show low toxicity, high water solubility, good chemical inertness, thermal stability, luminescence properties, and advantages of easy functionalization [12–14]. Biomass as a raw material being extensively used as a green source towards the synthesis of carbon dots very recently [15–18]. Most of the vegetables and fruits, vegetables and commonly found plants were used as carbon precursors for the synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials [19]. Due to the ease of green synthesis protocols and widely available raw materials, these CQDs have demonstrated excellent prospects for many of the technological applications such as food, medicine, environment, and energy storage [20–23]. Recently, several authors have reported the potential usage of CQD/GQDs for energy storage applications by fabricating supercapacitors [24,25], Li-ion batteries [26], fuel cells, and metal-air batteries [27], electrodes for electrocatalytic water splitting [28,29], reduction of CO2 [30] and biosensors [31] via facile low-cost synthesis techniques. The improved electronic properties of CQDs as electron donors-acceptors makes them potential for wide range of electronic applications such as fabrication of electrodes in energy storage devices [32,33]. The improved catalytic performance makes these CQDs as promising materials for next generation energy storage and energy conversion systems. Nevertheless, significant progress in electronic and optoelectronic applications of CQDs, the reports on synthesis of low cost CQDs via biological approach using Halimeda opuntia green algae with excellent optical and electrochemical properties have not been reported in the literature. For the first time we report biological synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Halimeda opuntia green algae as a material with excellent optical properties as well as superior electrochemical performance for possible energy storage applications such as fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors. The CQDs thus obtained were characterized for their physical and chemical properties through various analytical techniques such as HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR and UV-Vis spectroscopy. The photoluminescence properties were investigated through PL spectra. The electrochemical performance of modified GCE electrode using these CQDs were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-QD from Halimeda opuntia
ce:section.ce:para: The Carbon Quantum dots reported in this work were synthesized by pyrolysis method. As received Halimeda opuntia was crushed in a mixer grinder. Finely grinded algae powder (0.2 g) was transferred to the ceramic crucible and heated at a temperature of 500 °C in a muffle furnace for 3 h. The crucible was then allowed to cool to room temperature. The green coloured algae powder was carbonized into a black-grey product, this product was further dissolved in deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. The black-coloured suspension obtained was centrifuged for 15 min at 15,000 rpm to separate the large un-dissolved particles from the suspension. The resulting uniform suspension was then filtered through 100 nm pore size filter (PALL ACRO DISC, Japan) and subjected to the dialysis (using Float-A-Lyzer G2 Dialysis, Japan) for ultra-purification. These ultra-purified CQDs were transferred to a large glass vial and used for further experiments and characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots Modified Glassy Carbon Electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The glassy carbon electrode (GCE of diameter 5 mm) was polished with alumina slurries of 1 mm and 0.5 mm, followed by sonication with ethanol, acetone, and DI water, later dried at room temperature. The as-prepared carbon quantum dots catalyst (5 mg/mL) suspension was directly coated on the surface of GCE. This CQDs modified GCE electrode is taken as working electrode and is referred as CQDs/GCE.

Fluorescence spectroscopy analysis was performed on spectrofluorometer (JASCO FP-6500). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were analysed by JASCO instrument model V-570. The UV–visible spectroscopy analysis was done using spectrophotometer (UV-3600 spectrometer, Shimadzu, Japan). The morphology and size of CQDs were studied by high resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy with CQDs sample on carbon coated copper grids and HR-TEM (HR-TEM-JEOL model instrument 1200 EX, Japan). The structural features of CQDs were investigated through X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra (Bruker D8/Advance powder X-ray diffractometer). The atomic absorption analyses were performed using a Shimadzu AA-6300 instrument. The stability of CQDs was studied by particle size) analyser (Zetasizer Nano ZS-90, UK).

The electrochemical performance of as-prepared CQDs/GCE modified electrode (working electrode) was investigated for various electrochemical parameters such as cyclic voltammetry (CV) and charging-discharging cycles were performed using CHI 601C Electrochemical Workstation (CH Instruments). Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) and Pt wire electrode were taken as reference and counter electrodes respectively. Hereby, 6 M of KOH was prepared with DI water and used as an electrolyte in the electrochemical studies. The current response of the electrodes was normalized in accordance with the geometrical area of the working electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a) depicts the FTIR spectrum of the green algae and as synthesized CQDs from the green Algae of Halimeda opuntia.

FTIR spectrum revealed a broad and strong band cantered at 3432 cm−1, which could be attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric O-H stretching vibrations. This strong peak around 3400 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of CQDs synthesized from biological methods [34]. The C-H stretching of aliphatic hydrocarbons or aldehydes could be responsible for multiple vibrational peaks in the 3000–2500 cm−1 region. The CO bond of anhydride structures was assigned an absorption band at 1785 cm−1, while amide (-CONH-) groups were assigned a peak at 1484 cm−1. The peaks 1081 cm−1 and 711 cm−1 represents the C–O–C asymmetric stretching or C–H bending vibrations resulting from the methyl functional groups. Fig. 1(b) depicts the X-ray diffraction pattern of CQDs synthesized from the green Algae of Halimeda opuntia. The broad peak cantered around 2Ɵ = 25° is the characteristic peak of semi-crystalline CQDs synthesized form biomass as reported elsewhere [35].

Fig. 2 depicts the HR-TEM image of the synthesized CQDs from the green Algae of Halimeda opuntia. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 2a, b) at two different resolutions 50 nm and 20 nm reveals that the CQDs are spherical in shape with narrow size distribution (< 5 nm) and mono-dispersed. It is very similar to other reported work [36,37]. Finally, these findings demonstrated that CQDs average size< 5 nm were successfully synthesized using green algae of Halimeda opuntia. Further the Zeta potential of CQDs synthesized at 500 °C by pyrolysis is shown in Fig. 2(c). The Zeta potential result shows a strong peak corresponding to − 32 mV which suggests that the CQDs synthesized in this study are highly stable [38].

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of solution containing CQDs is depicted in Fig. 3(a). In the UV-Visible absorption spectrum, a broad absorption peak at 350 nm represents the transition from nonbonding orbital to antibonding pi orbitals (n-π*). The absorption band at 245 nm referred to the transitions of conjugated C-C units belongs to the carbon core [39]. Thambiraj and Shankaran synthesized carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse pulp, and they discovered an absorption peak at 283 nm, which they attribute to the π-π* transition for aromatic sp2 hybridization [37]. The formation of CQDs were confirmed by pale yellow solution during the day light and bright blue emission under the illumination of UV radiation, these observations were consistent with Nguyen et al. [40] who synthesized CQDs using biomass of banana peels.

The excitation and emission spectra of CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia green algae are shown in Fig. 3(b). The CQDs synthesized in the present investigations shows an excitation peak corresponds to a wavelength of 285 nm and an emission peak at 422 nm. Because of their larger surface area and quantum confinement effects the emission spectrum demonstrates the high fluorescence nature of the synthesized CQDs. The results are very comparable to those obtained with CQDs made from waste polystyrene foam soot, which was observed at excitation wavelengths of 305 and 385 nm [41]. The nearly constant emission spectra obtained for each excitation wavelength is due to the emission from the lowest single state of CQDs [42]. Furthermore, the fluorescence study was conducted at various emission wavelengths (Fig. 3c), and it was discovered that the emission wavelength increases with increasing emission intensity. According to Li et al. [43], CQDs with a broad absorption peak of 300 nm is attributed to the high energy level and complicated band structure [43]. CQD fluorescence emission spectra were observed at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 360 nm. The fluorescence spectra show a red shift from 390 nm to 430 nm that increases with increasing excitation wavelength. The strongest fluorescence appears in the synthesized CQDs with an increasing emission peak at 420 nm. According to Tang et al. [44], the CQDs observed in fluorescence spectra primarily emit blue, green, and red emissions. The excitation dependent emission spectra are mainly due to n → π* transition occurring through the non-bonding electrons of carboxylic and carbonyl groups of CQDs.

The electrochemical performance of CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia green nanostructure were analyzed by using CHI 601 C Electrochemical Workstation (CH Instruments) employing the Cyclic voltammograms (CV), Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) cycles, and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). We have employed three-electrode system with CQDs modified GCE as a working electrode using 6 M KOH as an electrolyte at ambient condition. Before the electrochemical analysis, all the samples were annealed at 250 °C for 3 h in argon atmosphere. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is one of the standard methods for determining the electrochemical properties in supercapacitors as it can distinguish between the behaviours of different kinds of SCs. The CV curves of CQDs/GCE electrode at different scanning rates (10–50 mV/s) are shown in Fig. 4(a). The typical CV curves shows pseudocapacitive behaviour due to Faradaic reactions, the broad redox followed by small separations between the peaks rather than a quasi-rectangular shaped curve. The broad redox peak observed in the CV curve indicates that the reduction process is happening inside the pseudocapacitive CQDs. The area under the curve increases with increasing scanning rates, which indicates superior specific capacitance of the CQDs/GCE electrode. The CV curves for different scan rates also indicates large current separation between the scans during forward and reverse conditions. The intensity of peak current also increases with the scan rate, indicating faster electronic and ionic charge transportation. The carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from biological routes have abundant functional groups that facilitates the electrochemical activity and significantly enhance the capacitance. The improved electrochemical performance of CV curves can be attributed to the mesoporous architecture of CQDs that facilitates ease of electron transport at the electrode surface. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed to understand the fundamental charge transfer kinetics in case of CQDs/GCE electrode. The Nyquist plot of CQDs/GCE electrode (Fig. 4b) indicates a very small charge-transfer resistance (Rct) indicating faster transportation of electronic and ionic charges at the electrode surface. The nearly linear impedance response of CQDs at low frequency region indicates its superior capacitive performance. The Nyquist plot of CQDs/GCE shown in Fig. 4(b) also indicates semi-circular behaviour in higher frequency region followed by nearly linear behaviour in low frequency region which is most important characteristic feature of high-performance supercapacitors based on carbon related quantum dots [45,46].

The GCD curves for CQDs/GCE electrode at a constant current density of 1 A g−1 for different charge-discharge cycles are shown in Fig. 5. The charge-discharge curves indicate constant potential for 10–200 cycles of operation reveals that the CQDs/GCE electrode is highly stable for different cycles of operation, which is a key parameter in fabrication of supercapacitor devices. This highly stable cycling performance also indicates superior capacitance retention ability of the CQDs/GCE electrode and long cycle life of the electrodes.

The variation of specific capacitance of CQDs as a function of current density is shown in Fig. 6(a). The CQDs shows highest specific capacitance Csp of 311 F g−1 at current density of 1 A g−1. The Csp decreases with current density in Fig. 6(a), which may be attributed to restricted migration of K+ ions and limited access to the active sites on the electrode surface. The electrochemical diffusion phenomena in CQD modified GCE can be further understood by the specific capacitance variation as a function of scan rate shown in Fig. 6(b). The specific capacitance of CQDs decreases with scan rate, which could be attributed to diffusion effects. At low scan rates there will be enough participation of active sites in electrochemical kinetics resulting into improved specific capacitance. At higher scan rates, the electrolyte fails to diffuse into the electrode surface within a given time and hence the capacitance decreases. Further Fig. 6(c) shows the variation of capacitance retention and coulombic efficiency as a function of cycles of operation. It is interesting to notice that the CQDs modified GCE shows excellent capacitance and coulombic efficiency upto 5000 cycles of operation. Hence this CQDs modified GCE has long cycling life. The energy storage performance (as supercapacitors) of these CQDs derived from Halimeda opuntia green algae has been compared with other carbon-based materials derived from biomass and are tabulated in Table 1. The CQDs synthesized in the present investigation shows superior specific capacitance compared to other carbon-based materials derived by biosynthesis methods as indicated in Table 1.

Due to excellent optical properties and superior electrochemical performance in terms of CV, cycling stability and low charge-transfer resistance, the CQDs presented in this study can be potentially utilized for the photo catalytical applications as well as energy storage applications such as fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors. To the best of our knowledge this is the first ever report on optical properties and electrochemical performance as energy storage devices on carbon quantum dots synthesized from Halimeda opuntia green algae.

For the first time we report biological synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Halimeda opuntia green algae as a material with excellent optical properties as well as superior electrochemical performance for possible energy storage applications such as fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors. The CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia were characterized by SEM, XRD, and FT-IR. The optical properties of CQDs in terms of UV-Vis spectra shows strong absorption in blue region where as PL spectra confirms excitation dependent emission with a red shift. The electrochemical performance of CQDs modified GCE electrode in terms of CV curves shows pseudocapacitive behavior. The charge-discharge cycling ability shows excellent stability indicating high capacitance retention behavior of the CQDs/GCE electrode. The semi-circular behavior at higher frequency followed by linear behavior and low charge-transfer resistance of the Nyquist plot indicates characteristic features of high-performance supercapacitors for CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia green algae. Due to promising optical and electrochemical performance the CQDs synthesized from low-cost biomass such as Halimeda opuntia algae can potentially be employed in photo catalytic and energy storage applications. Furthermore, a detailed experimental investigation on electrodes based on these CQDs synthesized from Halimeda opuntia are needed to understand the various parameters associated with the fabrication of supercapacitor devices.

All authors have contributed to the study conception and design. Material preparation, data collection and analysis were performed by [S.A.Al-Ghamdi], [Syed Khasim], [A.A.A Darwish], [Taymour A Hamdallah], [Mohamed E Elnair], [Ahmed Al-Atawi]. The first draft of the manuscript was written by [S.A Al-Ghamdi, Syed Khasim] and all authors commented on previous versions of the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

All the authors certify that, we adhere to ethical compliance in research, no part of the data has been published elsewhere. This research does not involve data related to any biological or animal research.
The authors would like to acknowledge financial support for this work, from the Deanship of Scientific research (DSR), University of Tabuk, Tabuk, Saudi Arabia, under Grant no. S-1442-0122.

All authors certify that they have no affiliations with or involvement in any organization or entity with any financial interest or non-financial interest in the subject matter or materials discussed in this manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
